content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
December 27, 1999
Phillip H. Romney
City of Santa Paula
970 Ventura Street
P.O. Box 569
Santa Paula, California 93061
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-263
Dear Mr. Romney:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Paula City
Councilmember Robin S. Sullivan regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) 1
QUESTION
May Councilmember Sullivan participate in the interview and appointment process for
commissioner positions on the Santa Paula Housing Authority?
CONCLUSION
Two applicants for commissioner were clients of Ms. Sullivan’s law firm. If either
applicant is a disqualifying source of income, Ms. Sullivan has a disqualifying conflict of interest
that prevents her from participating in the decisions to appoint commissioners to the Santa Paula
Housing Authority. Ms. Sullivan may only participate in the decisions ifher participation is
legally required and she is selected by random selection pursuant to the Commission’s regulation
implementing the legally required participation rule.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-263
Page 2
FACTS
Robin S. Sullivan is an elected member of the City Council for the City of Santa Paula.
Ms. Sullivan is an attorney; her husband and she own a private law firm in the City of Santa
Paula. They each have a 50 percent ownership interest.
Several vacancies currently exist on the Santa Paula Housing Authority (“Housing
Authority”). The Housing Authority is a separate governmental agency established in
accordance with California Health and Safety Code. Commissioners of the Housing Authority
are appointed by the Santa Paula City Council pursuant to the Health and Safety Code.
Several citizens ofthe City of Santa Paula have applied to the Santa Paula City Council
to be considered for appointment as successor commissioners to the Housing Authority. Two
applicants were clients ofMs. Sullivan’s law firm from whom she may have received
compensation equal to or in excess of $250 in the last 12 months.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.)
A public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official’s economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis for detennining
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The following advice applies that eight-step analysis to your question
regarding whether Ms. Sullivan may participate in the decisions of the city council to appoint
certain persons to the housing authority.
1. Public official.
As a member of the city council, Ms. Sullivan is a public official for purposes of the Act.
(Section 82048.)


File No. A-99-263
Page 3
2. Making, participating in making, or using official position to influence
governmental decisions.
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions apply only where the public official “make[s],
participate[s] in maldng, or in any way attempts to use his/her officialposition to influence a
governmental decision in which he/she knows or has reason to know he/she has a financial
interest.” (Section 87100, emphasis added.) The Commission has adopted a series ofregulations
which define “making,” “participating in making” and “influencing” a governmental dec,ision.
(Regulations 18702-18702.4.)
A city councilmember engages in the kinds of activities specified in Regulation 18702.1
for “making governmental decisions” by voting on an appointment. (Regulation 18702.l(a)(2).)
Participating in interviews and deliberating on appointment matters constitute “participating in
making” a governmental decision under Regulation 18702.2. In addition, attempting to privately
or publicly sway the council’s appointment decision constitutes “influencing a governmental
decision” under Regulation 18702.3.
3. Identifying Ms. Sullivan’s economic interest(s).
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) Section 87103 provides as follows:
“A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member ofhis
or her immediate family, or on any ofthe following:
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official


File No. A-99-263
Page 4
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (f) of
Section 89503....”
Ms. Sullivan and her husband are the sole owners oftheir law practice and each has a
50 percent interest in the business. Under the Act, a public official’s income includes his/her pro
rata share of income received by a business entity ofwhich he/she owns 10 percent or more.
(Section 87103.) Income also includes any community property interest in the income of a
spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Since Ms. Sullivan and her spouse own the law firm, her pro rata
share of income to the firm is her 50 percent ownership interest, plus one-half ofher spouse’s
interest. Under these facts, the disqualifying amount to Ms. Sullivan from a single client is $333.
(See King Advice Letter, No. 1-99-145, copy enclosed.) The $333 figure results in a $250
amount attributable to Ms. Sullivan for purposes of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
(approximately $167 attributed to the councilmember as half owner and $83 attributed to her as
community property).
We do not know if either client/applicant is a disqualifying source of income of $250 or
more within the last 12-month period. Therefore, please ascertain how much each client/
applicant paid the law firm, and use the $333 amount given in the King letter to determine if
either client is a disqualifying source of income of $250 or more to Ms. Suffivan.
4. Determining whether Ms. Suffivan’s economic interests are directly or indirectly
involved in any decision she will make, participate in or influence.
Determining whether the public official’s economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision (Regulation 1 8700(b)(4)) is important because it helps
determine which materiality test to use in deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interest.
Regulation 18704.1 sets forth the directly/indirectly involved test for sources of income.
Subdivision (a) ofthis regulation states that a source of income will be directly involved in a
governmental decision if it:
“(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official’s
agency....”
Two clients ofMs. Sullivan’s law firm have applied to the city council for a housing
authority commissioner position, thereby initiating the city council’s consideration process.


File No. A-99-263
Page 5
These persons will be considered the subject ofa decision by the city council. Therefore, the
clients meet both subsections of Regulation 18704.1(a) and will be directly involved in the city
council decisions concerning appointments to the housing authority.
2
5. Determining the applicable materiality standard.
Regulation 18705.3 sets forth the materiality test pertaining to persons who are sources of
income to the official. With respect to directly involved sources of income, the regulation states,
“[ajny reasonably foreseeable fmancial effect on a person who is a source ofincome to a public
official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official’s agency, is deemed
material.” (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
6. Using the materiality standard to decide if the appointment decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the applicants who are sources of
income to Ms. Sullivan.
The next question is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the appointment decisions
(i.e., interviews, deliberations and votes) will have at least some financial effect on the applicants
who may be sources of income to Ms. Sullivan. (Regulation 18700(b)(6), 18706.) A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood
that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 198.) On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.
It is reasonably foreseeable that obtaining an appointment (or not obtaining an
appointment) as a housing authority commissioner will have at least some financial effect on
whomever is selected. Therefore, if either client is a source of income, Ms. Sullivan has a
conflict, unless the public generally exception applies.
7. Is the effect of the appointment decisions on Ms. Sullivan distinguishable from
the effect of the decisions on the public generally?
Ms. Sullivan’s possible conflict derives from the financial effect of the appointment
decisions on two applicants who may be sources of income to her. For the public generally
exception to apply, a significant segment ofthe public would have to be affected by the
appointment decision in substantially the same manner as the two applicants. (Regulation
18707(b).) Since this appears unlikely, the public generally exception does not apply.
2 This further analysis is dependent upon a finding that one/both clients are a source of income of $250 or
more in the last 12 months to Ms. Sullivan, as discussed above.


File No. A-99-263
Page 6
Since the appointment decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on two ofMs. Sullivan’s firm’s clients who will be competing for the commissioner
positions, and because her interest is distinct from that held by the public generally, Ms. Sullivan
would have a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding the decisions to appoint the
commissioners and she may not participate in those decisions.
8. Is Ms. Suffivan’s participation in the decisions legally required?
You have determined that two other members of the city council have a conflict of
interest. One ofthese members has a conflict under Government Code Section 1090 and cannot
participate at all, including the random selection process. IfMs. Sullivan also has a conflict of
interest, there are only two members eligible to participate. To illustrate the process, we will
assume that Ms. Sullivan has a conflict. Therefore, she and one of the disqualified members
would participate in the random selection process to produce a quorum of three councilmembers.
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
governmental decision to participate in the decision when the official’s participation is legally
required. The rule does not apply when there is an alternative source of decisionmaking
consistent with the statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708, copy enclosed.) Thus, it
only applies when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of
the disqualified official.
3 Consequently, it does not apply when the disqualified official’s vote is
merely needed to break a tie or when a quorum can be convened of other members of the city
council who are not disqualified, whether or not such other members are actually present at the
time of the disqualification.
Under this rule, not all officials who are disqualified may participate. The legally
required participation rule is construed narrowly. (Regulation 18708(c).) Consequently, the rule
only allows the participation ofthe fewest number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute
a quorum. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) The best random method of selecting which
disqualified member should participate is by lot. Other means ofrandom selection that are
impartial and equitable may also be used. Whatever method is used, all disqualified officials
must participate in the random selection and all must be equally likely to be chosen. (Heisinger
Advice Letter, No. A-95-333.)
Once it is determined which disqualified official will participate in a decision, that
official is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters unless an official
previously disqualified no longer has a financial interest in the decision. A disqualified official
who participates under the authority of Section 87101 may participate fully in the matter,
You have asked whether we agree with your conclusion that no alternative source of decision exists
under these circumstances. Whether it is legally impossible for the council to act depends upon the interpretation of
laws outside the jurisdiction of the FPPC. We can only advise you of the legal standards under the Act.


File No. A-99-263
Page 7
including taking part in deliberations and voting in open sessions of the body and in such closed
sessions as are required by law. However, the reinstated official may not attempt to influence the
outcome of the matter “behind the scenes” by engaging in private discussions with other
members or staff. (Regulation 18708(b)(4); Grunwald Advice Letter, No. A-95-184.)
When a disqualified public official is legally required to participate, the official must
disclose on the record the existence of the financial interest, describe with particularity the nature
ofthe interest and state the reason why there is no alternative source of decisionma.king.,
Ms. Sullivan may have a conflict and therefore must disqualify herself from participation
for the reasons set forth above. If a quorum of the remaining councilmembers cannot be
achieved without using persons previously disqualified, and ifno other method of
clecisionmaking exists, a selection of one or more (as needed to constitute a quorum) disqualified
councilmembers may be accomplished pursuant to the random selection method described above.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
Q
L1L/jQ-
By:
Jill Stecher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

implementing the legally required participation rule.Page 2

must participate in the random selection and all must be equally likely to be chosen. (Heisinger

QUESTION: May Councilmember Sullivan participate in the interview and appointment process for
commissioner positions on the Santa Paula Housing Authority?

CONCLUSION: Two applicants for commissioner were clients of Ms. Sullivan’s law firm. If either
applicant is a disqualifying source of income, Ms. Sullivan has a disqualifying conflict of interest

Housing Authority. Ms. Sullivan may only participate in the decisions ifher participation is
legally required and she is selected by random selection pursuant to the Commission’s regulation

Sullivan regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1 QUESTION May Councilmember Sullivan participate in the interview and appointment process for commissioner positions on the Santa Paula Housing Authority? Ms. Sullivan may only participate in the decisions ifher participation is legally required and she is selected by random selection pursuant to the Commission’s regulation implementing the legally required participation rule.
